Variables & Types.desc: Get the type of a variable
Variables & Types.details: Returns the class type of the given object. Useful for debugging and ensuring data matches expected formats.
Converts a value to a specific data type. Basic building block for handling user input or API data.

Variables & Types.desc: Get the length of a string, list, or dictionary
Variables & Types.details: Returns the number of items in an object. Works with sequences (string, bytes, tuple, list, or range) or collections (dictionary, set, or frozen set).

Strings.details: Returns a copy of the string with all cased characters converted to lowercase or uppercase. Original string remains unchanged.
Returns a copy of the string with the leading and trailing characters removed (default is whitespace).

Strings.desc: Split string into a list by separator
Strings.details: Returns a list of the words in the string, using the specified separator as the delimiter string.

Strings.desc: Join a list of strings into a single string
Strings.details: Returns a string which is the concatenation of the strings in the iterable. The separator between elements is the string providing this method.
Returns a copy of the string with all occurrences of substring 'old' replaced by 'new'.
String literals that are prefixed with 'f' or 'F' and contain expressions inside curly braces. Introduced in Python 3.6.

Lists.desc: Add an item to the end of the list
Lists.details: Adds a single item to the end of the list. Modifies the list in-place and returns None.

Lists.desc: Insert an item at a given position
Lists.details: Inserts an element at a given index. The first argument is the index of the element before which to insert.

Lists.desc: Remove and return an item (default is last)
Lists.details: Removes the item at the given position in the list, and returns it. If no index is specified, removes and returns the last item.

Lists.desc: Remove the first item with the specified value
Lists.details: Removes the first item from the list whose value is equal to x. It raises a ValueError if there is no such item.

Lists.desc: Sort the items of the list in place
Lists.details: Sorts the items of the list in place (the arguments can be used for sort customization).
Returns a new list that is a reversed copy of the original list using slice notation [start:stop:step].

Lists.cmd: [x for x in lst if x > 0]
Lists.details: A concise way to create lists. Consists of brackets containing an expression followed by a for clause, then zero or more for or if clauses.

Dictionaries.desc: Return a view of the dictionary's keys
Dictionaries.details: Returns a dynamic view object of all the keys in the dictionary. Useful for iterating or checking existence.

Dictionaries.desc: Return a view of the dictionary's values
Dictionaries.details: Returns a dynamic view object of all the values in the dictionary.

Dictionaries.desc: Return a view of the dictionary's (key, value) pairs
Dictionaries.details: Returns a dynamic view object of all the key-value pairs in the dictionary as tuples.
Dictionaries.example: for k, v in d.items(): print(k, v)

Dictionaries.desc: Return value for key, or default if key not found
Dictionaries.details: Returns the value for key if key is in the dictionary, else default. Never raises a KeyError.

Dictionaries.desc: Remove specified key and return the corresponding value
Dictionaries.details: Removes the key from the dictionary and returns its value. Raises KeyError if key is not found and no default is provided.

Dictionaries.desc: Update dictionary with key/value pairs from d2
Dictionaries.details: Update the dictionary with the key/value pairs from d2, overwriting existing keys.

Control Flow.details: Executes a block of code if the specified condition is true.
Used after an 'if' statement to check another condition if the previous conditions were false.
Executes a block of code if none of the preceding 'if' or 'elif' conditions are true.
Iterates over the members of a sequence in order, executing the block for each item.
Common way to iterate a specific number of times using the range() function.
Control Flow.example: for i in range(5): print(i)  # 0 to 4

Control Flow.details: Repeatedly executes a target statement as long as a given condition is true.

Functions.details: Creates a reusable block of code that only runs when it is called. Can take arguments and return values.
Small anonymous functions can be created with the lambda keyword. They are restricted to a single expression.
Functions.example:
square = lambda x: x * x
print(square(5))  # 25

Functions.details: Used in function definitions to pass a variable-length argument list as a tuple.
Used in function definitions to pass a keyworded, variable-length argument list as a dictionary.

File Handling.desc: Open file safely using context manager
File Handling.details: The 'with' statement ensures that the file is properly closed after its suite finishes, even if an exception is raised.

File Handling.desc: Read entire file into a string
File Handling.details: Reads the entire content of the file and returns it as a single string.

File Handling.desc: Read file into a list of lines
File Handling.details: Reads all the lines of a file and returns them as a list of strings.

File Handling.desc: Write a string to the file
File Handling.details: Writes the string to the file and returns the number of characters written.

Environment & Packages.details: Creates a self-contained directory tree that contains a Python installation for a particular version of Python, plus a number of additional packages.
Activates the virtual environment in the current shell session on Unix-based systems.
Activates the virtual environment in the current shell session on Windows.
Downloads and installs a package from PyPI (Python Package Index) or other sources.

Environment & Packages.desc: Save installed packages to a file
Environment & Packages.details: Outputs installed packages in requirements format, typically used to share project dependencies.
